title: Score-Based Generative Modeling through Stochastic Differential Equations

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## SCORE-BASED GENERATIVE MODELING THROUGH STOCHASTIC DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS
**Yang Song**[˚] **Jascha Sohl-Dickstein** Stanford University Google Brain yangsong@cs.stanford.edu jaschasd@google.com

**Diederik P. Kingma** Google Brain durk@google.com

**Abhishek Kumar Stefano Ermon Ben Poole** Google Brain Stanford University Google Brain abhishk@google.com ermon@cs.stanford.edu pooleb@google.com

## ABSTRACT

Creating noise from data is easy; creating data from noise is generative modeling. We present a stochastic differential equation (SDE) that smoothly transforms a complex data distribution to a known prior distribution by slowly injecting noise, and a corresponding reverse-time SDE that transforms the prior distribution back into the data distribution by slowly removing the noise. Crucially, the reverse-time SDE depends only on the time-dependent gradient field (a.k.a., score) of the perturbed data distribution. By leveraging advances in score-based generative modeling, we can accurately estimate these scores with neural networks, and use numerical SDE solvers to generate samples. We show that this framework encapsulates previous approaches in score-based generative modeling and diffusion probabilistic modeling, allowing for new sampling procedures and new modeling capabilities. In particular, we introduce a predictor-corrector framework to correct errors in the evolution of the discretized reverse-time SDE. We also derive an equivalent neural ODE that samples from the same distribution as the SDE, but additionally enables exact likelihood computation, and improved sampling efficiency. In addition, we provide a new way to solve inverse problems with score-based models, as demonstrated with experiments on class-conditional generation, image inpainting, and colorization. Combined with multiple architectural improvements, we achieve record-breaking performance for unconditional image generation on CIFAR-10 with an Inception score of 9.89 and FID of 2.20, a competitive likelihood of 2.99 bits/dim, and demonstrate high fidelity generation of 1024 ˆ 1024 images for the first time from a score-based generative model.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Two successful classes of probabilistic generative models involve sequentially corrupting training data with slowly increasing noise, and then learning to reverse this corruption in order to form a generative model of the data. _Score matching with Langevin dynamics_ (SMLD) (Song & Ermon, 2019) estimates the _score_ ( _i.e_ ., the gradient of the log probability density with respect to data) at each noise scale, and then uses Langevin dynamics to sample from a sequence of decreasing noise scales during generation. _Denoising diffusion probabilistic modeling_ (DDPM) (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015; Ho et al., 2020) trains a sequence of probabilistic models to reverse each step of the noise corruption, using knowledge of the functional form of the reverse distributions to make training tractable. For continuous state spaces, the DDPM training objective implicitly computes scores at each noise scale. We therefore refer to these two model classes together as _score-based generative models_ .

Score-based generative models, and related techniques (Bordes et al., 2017; Goyal et al., 2017; Du & Mordatch, 2019), have proven effective at generation of images (Song & Ermon, 2019; 2020; Ho et al., 2020), audio (Chen et al., 2020; Kong et al., 2020), graphs (Niu et al., 2020), and shapes (Cai

et al., 2020). To enable new sampling methods and further extend the capabilities of score-based generative models, we propose a unified framework that generalizes previous approaches through the lens of stochastic differential equations (SDEs).

Specifically, instead of perturbing data with a finite number of noise distributions, we consider a continuum of distributions that evolve over time according to a diffusion process. This process progressively diffuses a data point into random noise, and is given by a prescribed SDE that does not depend on the data and has no trainable parameters. By reversing this process, we can smoothly mold random noise into data for sample generation. Crucially, this reverse process satisfies a reverse-time SDE (Anderson, 1982), which can be derived from the forward SDE given the score of the marginal probability densities as a function of time. We can therefore approximate the reverse-time SDE by training a time-dependent neural network to estimate the scores, and then produce samples using numerical SDE solvers. Our key idea is summarized in Fig. 1.

Our proposed framework has several theoretical and practical contributions:

**Flexible sampling and likelihood computation:** We can employ any general-purpose SDE solver to integrate the reverse-time SDE for sampling. In addition, we propose two special methods not viable for general SDEs: (i) Predictor-Corrector (PC) samplers that combine numerical SDE solvers with score-based MCMC approaches, such as Langevin MCMC (Parisi, 1981) and HMC (Neal et al., 2011); and (ii) deterministic samplers based on the probability flow ordinary differential equation (ODE). The former _unifies and improves_ over existing sampling methods for score-based models. The latter allows for _fast adaptive sampling_ via black-box ODE solvers, _flexible data manipulation_ via latent codes, a _uniquely identifiable encoding_ , and notably, _exact likelihood computation_ .

**Controllable generation:** We can modulate the generation process by conditioning on information not available during training, because the conditional reverse-time SDE can be efficiently estimated from _unconditional_ scores. This enables applications such as class-conditional generation, image inpainting, colorization and other inverse problems, all achievable using a single unconditional score-based model without re-training.

**Unified framework:** Our framework provides a unified way to explore and tune various SDEs for improving score-based generative models. The methods of SMLD and DDPM can be amalgamated into our framework as discretizations of two separate SDEs. Although DDPM (Ho et al., 2020) was recently reported to achieve higher sample quality than SMLD (Song & Ermon, 2019; 2020), we show that with better architectures and new sampling algorithms allowed by our framework, the latter can catch up—it achieves new state-of-the-art Inception score (9.89) and FID score (2.20) on CIFAR-10, as well as high-fidelity generation of 1024 ˆ 1024 images for the first time from a score-based model. In addition, we propose a new SDE under our framework that achieves a likelihood value of 2.99 bits/dim on uniformly dequantized CIFAR-10 images, setting a new record on this task.

## 3 SCORE-BASED GENERATIVE MODELING WITH SDES

Perturbing data with multiple noise scales is key to the success of previous methods. We propose to generalize this idea further to an infinite number of noise scales, such that perturbed data distributions evolve according to an SDE as the noise intensifies. An overview of our framework is given in Fig. 2.

## 3.1 PERTURBING DATA WITH SDES

In other words, _p_ 0 is the data distribution and _pT_ is the prior distribution. This diffusion process can be modeled as the solution to an Itˆo SDE:

## 4.1 GENERAL-PURPOSE NUMERICAL SDE SOLVERS

Numerical solvers provide approximate trajectories from SDEs. Many general-purpose numerical methods exist for solving SDEs, such as Euler-Maruyama and stochastic Runge-Kutta methods (Kloeden & Platen, 2013), which correspond to different discretizations of the stochastic dynamics. We can apply any of them to the reverse-time SDE for sample generation.

Ancestral sampling, the sampling method of DDPM (Eq. (4)), actually corresponds to one special discretization of the reverse-time VP SDE (Eq. (11)) (see Appendix E). Deriving the ancestral sampling rules for new SDEs, however, can be non-trivial. To remedy this, we propose _reverse diffusion samplers_ (details in Appendix E), which discretize the reverse-time SDE in the same way as the forward one, and thus can be readily derived given the forward discretization. As shown in Table 1, reverse diffusion samplers perform slightly better than ancestral sampling for both SMLD and DDPM models on CIFAR-10 (DDPM-type ancestral sampling is also applicable to SMLD models, see Appendix F.)

## 4.2 PREDICTOR-CORRECTOR SAMPLERS

Unlike generic SDEs, we have additional information that can be used to improve solutions. Since we have a score-based model **s** _**θ**_ ˚p **x** _, t_ q « _∇_ **x** log _pt_ p **x** q, we can employ score-based MCMC approaches, such as Langevin MCMC (Parisi, 1981; Grenander & Miller, 1994) or HMC (Neal et al., 2011) to sample from _pt_ directly, and correct the solution of a numerical SDE solver.

Specifically, at each time step, the numerical SDE solver first gives an estimate of the sample at the next time step, playing the role of a “predictor”. Then, the score-based MCMC approach corrects the marginal distribution of the estimated sample, playing the role of a “corrector”. The idea is analogous to Predictor-Corrector methods, a family of numerical continuation techniques for solving systems of equations (Allgower & Georg, 2012), and we similarly name our hybrid sampling algorithms _Predictor-Corrector_ (PC) samplers. Please find pseudo-code and a complete description in Appendix G. PC samplers generalize the original sampling methods of SMLD and DDPM: the former uses an identity function as the predictor and annealed Langevin dynamics as the corrector, while the latter uses ancestral sampling as the predictor and identity as the corrector.

We test PC samplers on SMLD and DDPM models (see Algorithms 2 and 3 in Appendix G) trained with original discrete objectives given by Eqs. (1) and (3). This exhibits the compatibility of PC samplers to score-based models trained with a fixed number of noise scales. We summarize the performance of different samplers in Table 1, where probability flow is a predictor to be discussed in Section 4.3. Detailed experimental settings and additional results are given in Appendix G. We observe that our reverse diffusion sampler always outperform ancestral sampling, and corrector-only methods (C2000) perform worse than other competitors (P2000, PC1000) with the same computation (In fact, we need way more corrector steps per noise scale, and thus more computation, to match the performance of other samplers.) For all predictors, adding one corrector step for each predictor step (PC1000) doubles computation but always improves sample quality (against P1000). Moreover, it is typically better than doubling the number of predictor steps without adding a corrector (P2000), where we have to interpolate between noise scales in an ad hoc manner (detailed in Appendix G) for SMLD/DDPM models. ## 4.3 PROBABILITY FLOW AND CONNECTION TO NEURAL ODES

**Exact likelihood computation** Leveraging the connection to neural ODEs, we can compute the density defined by Eq. (13) via the instantaneous change of variables formula (Chen et al., 2018). This allows us to compute the _exact likelihood on any input data_ (details in Appendix D.2). ## 4.4 ARCHITECTURE IMPROVEMENTS

We explore several new architecture designs for score-based models using both VE and VP SDEs (details in Appendix H), where we train models with the same discrete objectives as in SMLD/DDPM. We directly transfer the architectures for VP SDEs to sub-VP SDEs due to their similarity. Our optimal architecture for the VE SDE, named NCSN++, achieves an FID of 2.45 on CIFAR-10 with PC samplers, while our optimal architecture for the VP SDE, called DDPM++, achieves 2.78.

By switching to the continuous training objective in Eq. (7), and increasing the network depth, we can further improve sample quality for all models. The resulting architectures are denoted as NCSN++ cont. and DDPM++ cont. in Table 3 for VE and VP/sub-VP SDEs respectively. Results reported in Table 3 are for the checkpoint with the smallest FID over the course of training, where samples are generated with PC samplers. In contrast, FID scores and NLL values in Table 2 are reported for the last training checkpoint, and samples are obtained with black-box ODE solvers. As shown in Table 3, VE SDEs typically provide better sample quality than VP/sub-VP SDEs, but we also empirically observe that their likelihoods are worse than VP/sub-VP SDE counterparts. This indicates that practitioners likely need to experiment with different SDEs for varying domains and architectures.

Our best model for sample quality, NCSN++ cont. (deep, VE), doubles the network depth and sets new records for both inception score and FID on unconditional generation for CIFAR-10. Surprisingly, we can achieve better FID than the previous best conditional generative model without requiring labeled data. With all improvements together, we also obtain the first set of high-fidelity samples on CelebA-HQ 1024 ˆ 1024 from score-based models (see Appendix H.3). Our best model for likelihoods, DDPM++ cont. (deep, sub-VP), similarly doubles the network depth and achieves a log-likelihood of 2.99 bits/dim with the continuous objective in Eq. (7). To our best knowledge, this is the highest likelihood on uniformly dequantized CIFAR-10.

In some cases, it is possible to train a separate model to learn the forward process log _pt_ p **y** | **x** p _t_ qq and compute its gradient. Otherwise, we may estimate the gradient with heuristics and domain knowledge. In Appendix I.4, we provide a broadly applicable method for obtaining such an estimate without the need of training auxiliary models.

We consider three applications of controllable generation with this approach: class-conditional generation, image imputation and colorization. When **y** represents class labels, we can train a time-dependent classifier _pt_ p **y** | **x** p _t_ qq for class-conditional sampling. Since the forward SDE is tractable, we can easily create training data p **x** p _t_ q _,_ **y** q for the time-dependent classifier by first sampling p **x** p0q _,_ **y** q from a dataset, and then sampling **x** p _t_ q „ _p_ 0 _t_ p **x** p _t_ q | **x** p0qq. Afterwards, we may employ a mixture of cross-entropy losses over different time steps, like Eq. (7), to train the time-dependent classifier _pt_ p **y** | **x** p _t_ qq. Colorization is a special case of imputation, except that the known data dimensions are coupled. We can decouple these data dimensions with an orthogonal linear transformation, and perform imputation in the transformed space (details in Appendix I.3). Fig. 4 (right) shows results for inpainting and colorization achieved with unconditional time-dependent score-based models.

## 6 CONCLUSION

We presented a framework for score-based generative modeling based on SDEs. Our work enables a better understanding of existing approaches, new sampling algorithms, exact likelihood computation, uniquely identifiable encoding, latent code manipulation, and brings new conditional generation abilities to the family of score-based generative models.

While our proposed sampling approaches improve results and enable more efficient sampling, they remain slower at sampling than GANs (Goodfellow et al., 2014) on the same datasets. Identifying ways of combining the stable learning of score-based generative models with the fast sampling of implicit models like GANs remains an important research direction. Additionally, the breadth of samplers one can use when given access to score functions introduces a number of hyper-parameters. Future work would benefit from improved methods to automatically select and tune these hyperparameters, as well as more extensive investigation on the merits and limitations of various samplers.

coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3-flash-preview)
coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper presents a unified continuous-time framework for score-based generative modeling using stochastic differential equations (SDEs). By generalizing discrete diffusion and score-matching methods into a single SDE-based formulation, the authors enable new sampling techniques like Predictor-Corrector methods and deterministic probability flow ODEs for exact likelihood estimation.

The paper is a significant contribution that unifies two major branches of generative modeling and provides a rigorous mathematical foundation for diffusion processes. It achieves state-of-the-art results on image generation benchmarks and introduces versatile tools like the probability flow ODE for controllable generation.

Overview issues:

- Discretization Bias in Reverse-Time SDE Solvers: The transition from continuous-time SDEs in Section 3 to numerical implementations in Section 4 introduces potential discretization errors that are not fully analyzed. While the paper proposes Predictor-Corrector (PC) samplers to mitigate these errors, it lacks a formal discussion on how the discretization step size affects the convergence of the generated distribution to the true data distribution. If the score network has approximation errors, these can accumulate over the 1000+ steps typically used in Table 1. The authors should provide a bound or empirical analysis of the cumulative error relative to the discretization step size to ensure that the numerical solution remains faithful to the reverse SDE's theoretical properties.

- Weighting Function Heuristics and Likelihood Optimization: In Section 3.3, the weighting function lambda(t) in Equation 7 is chosen based on a heuristic proportional to the inverse of the expected squared norm of the score. While this choice is common in the literature to balance different noise scales, the paper does not theoretically justify why this specific weighting is optimal for the exact likelihood results reported in Table 2. Since the probability flow ODE allows for exact NLL computation, there is a disconnect between the score-matching training objective and the maximum likelihood evaluation metric. A more rigorous treatment would involve showing how lambda(t) relates to the variational lower bound or describing the trade-offs when the objective is not explicitly maximizing likelihood.

- Score Estimation in Low-Density Regions: The framework relies on the score network accurately estimating the gradient of the log-density across the entire diffusion path. However, as noted in Section 3.2, the reverse SDE depends on the score of the perturbed data distribution, which becomes poorly defined in regions of low data density at small noise levels (t near 0). The paper assumes the score network generalizes well to these regions, but the training objective in Equation 7 is expectation-based and may under-sample these critical areas. This can lead to instabilities or 'manifold-drifting' during the final stages of sampling. The authors should include an analysis of score estimation accuracy at varying time steps, particularly for low-noise regimes.

- Computational Overhead of Predictor-Corrector Framework: The Predictor-Corrector samplers introduced in Section 4.2 significantly improve sample quality but at a high computational cost, often doubling the number of function evaluations (NFEs) compared to simple SDE solvers. Table 1 shows that PC1000 outperforms P2000 in some metrics, yet the paper lacks a systematic study on the optimal ratio of predictor to corrector steps. For high-dimensional data like the 1024x1024 images mentioned in the abstract, this overhead becomes a bottleneck. The paper would be more complete with a discussion on the scaling laws of PC samplers and whether the corrector step remains efficient as the data dimension increases.

- Regularity Conditions for Reverse SDE Existence: The derivation of the reverse-time SDE in Equation 6 cites Anderson (1982), which requires certain regularity conditions on the drift and diffusion coefficients, as well as the smoothness of the probability density p_t(x). In Section 3.1, the authors assume the coefficients are globally Lipschitz, but they do not explicitly verify if the data distributions encountered in deep learning (which often sit on low-dimensional manifolds) satisfy the necessary smoothness for the score to exist everywhere. If the data distribution is singular, the score is undefined. The paper should clarify the assumptions made about the support of p_0 and how the initial noise injection ensures the existence of the score for all t > 0.

- Missing verification of the probability flow ODE recovery of discrete cases: The paper claims that the probability flow ODE in Equation (13) provides a deterministic process that samples from the same marginals as the SDE. While the authors state that SMLD and DDPM are discretizations of specific SDEs, they do not formally demonstrate that the probability flow ODE recovers known deterministic limits of these models, such as the Denoising Diffusion Implicit Model (DDIM). To ensure the framework is truly unified, the authors should compute the ODE for the VP SDE (Equation 11) and show that its discretization matches the DDIM update rule. This would bridge the gap between their continuous theory and the existing literature on deterministic sampling.

- Lack of simulation on a toy non-Gaussian distribution: The paper relies heavily on high-dimensional image benchmarks like CIFAR-10, but lacks a controlled simulation on a low-dimensional, non-Gaussian distribution where the ground truth score is known. For a theory-heavy paper introducing a new SDE framework, it is standard to show that the reverse-time SDE and the probability flow ODE can accurately recover a simple distribution, such as a 2D mixture of Gaussians or a 'Swiss roll.' Computing the score error and the KL divergence between the generated and true distributions for these cases would confirm that the results in Table 2 are not solely due to architectural improvements in the neural networks.

- Underdeveloped implementation guidance for the sub-VP SDE: The sub-VP SDE is introduced as a novel contribution that achieves record likelihoods, yet the paper provides little intuition or practical guidance on why this specific functional form (Equation 12) outperforms the standard VP SDE. The text mentions that its variance is always bounded by the VP SDE, but it does not explain how this property translates to easier score estimation or better likelihoods. The paper needs a more detailed discussion or a comparative plot of the variance schedules and their corresponding score magnitudes to help practitioners understand when to prefer sub-VP over VP.

- Missing discussion on the curse of dimensionality in conditional scores: Section 5 claims that controllable generation can be achieved using the gradient of the log-likelihood of a classifier, yet it ignores the practical difficulty of estimating these gradients in high-dimensional spaces. As the dimension of x increases, the classifier p_t(y|x) is likely to become overconfident, leading to 'shattered' gradients or vanishing signals that can derail the reverse SDE. The paper should discuss how to handle the scale of the conditional gradient term in Equation (14) relative to the unconditional score, perhaps by suggesting a specific schedule for a guidance scale parameter, which is common in related conditional diffusion work.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Provide a formal analysis or empirical study on the relationship between discretization step size and error accumulation in the reverse SDE.

- Justify the choice of the weighting function lambda(t) in Equation 7 relative to the likelihood results in Table 2.

- Add a discussion on the regularity conditions of the data distribution and how the SDE framework handles potentially non-smooth or singular densities.

- Include a sensitivity analysis for the Predictor-Corrector ratio to guide hyperparameter selection for different data dimensions.

comments.explanation: The expression inside the square root for the diffusion coefficient in Equation (12) is mathematically malformed. The term involving 't' and 't_0' appears to be a typesetting error intended to represent an integral from 0 to t, likely \int_0^t. In its current form, the variable 's' is introduced in the exponent without a binding integral operator, making the SDE ill-defined. This must be corrected to ensure the sub-VP SDE is mathematically sound and reproducible.

comments.title: Incomplete Condition for SDE Strong Solution Existence
comments.quote: The SDE has a unique strong solution as long as the coefficients are globally Lipschitz in both state and time (Øksendal, 2003).
comments.explanation: The claim that global Lipschitz continuity is sufficient for a unique strong solution is incomplete. Standard results in stochastic calculus (e.g., Øksendal, 2003, Theorem 5.2.1) require both the Lipschitz condition and a linear growth condition: |f(x,t)| + |g(t)| <= C(1 + |x|). While Lipschitz continuity implies linear growth in the state variable x for a fixed t, it does not guarantee it across the entire time interval [0, T] unless the coefficients are bounded at a specific point for all t. Since the VE SDE involves a noise schedule that can grow rapidly, explicitly stating the linear growth requirement is necessary to ensure the solution does not explode.

comments.title: Inconsistency in ODE Drift for General Multivariate SDEs
comments.quote: \mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} = \left[ \mathbf {f} (\mathbf {x}, t) - \frac {1}{2} g (t) ^ {2} \nabla_ {\mathbf {x}} \log p _ {t} (\mathbf {x}) \right] \mathrm {d} t, \tag {13}
comments.explanation: The paper claims this ODE holds for 'all diffusion processes,' but Equation (13) assumes a scalar diffusion coefficient g(t). For a general multivariate SDE where the diffusion is a matrix G(t), the drift of the probability flow ODE must be f - 0.5 * G(t)G(t)^T * grad log p_t. If G(t) is not a scalar multiple of the identity, the g(t)^2 term is incorrect. The equation should be updated to the matrix form to maintain the claimed generality of the framework.

comments.explanation: The text references Equation (7) as a template for the classifier loss, but Equation (7) is a weighted Fisher Divergence (a regression objective for vector fields). A cross-entropy loss for a classifier is a scalar-valued log-likelihood objective. While the time-weighting concept is shared, the functional form of Equation (7) does not apply to categorical labels. The sentence should be revised to clarify that the classifier uses a time-weighted cross-entropy objective rather than implying it follows the score-matching form.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Reverse-Time SDE Differential Notation
comments.quote: $\bar{\mathbf{w}}$  is a standard Wiener process when time flows backwards from  $T$  to 0, and  $\mathrm{d}t$  is an infinitesimal negative timestep. Once
comments.explanation: Defining dt as an 'infinitesimal negative timestep' while using the standard additive form in Equation (6) creates a sign ambiguity. In the forward SDE, dt is positive. If Equation (6) is integrated from T to 0 with dt < 0, the drift term would result in a direction opposite to the one required by the Anderson (1982) derivation. It is more standard to define the reverse SDE using a positive time increment for a forward-running auxiliary time variable, or to clarify that the integral handles the sign of the differential.

comments.title: Inaccurate Weighting Proportionality for DDPM
comments.quote: )$. Notably, the weights of the $i$-th summand in Eq. (1) and Eq. (3), namely $\sigma_{i}^{2}$ and $(1-\alpha_{i})$, are related to corresponding perturbation kernels in the same functional

The phrasing 'related in the same functional form' suggests a heuristic similarity when it is a direct consequence of the Gaussian kernel variance. coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
This paper presents a foundational and highly influential framework that unifies score-based generative modeling and diffusion models through stochastic differential equations. It makes significant theoretical and practical contributions, including a continuous-time formulation, new sampling methods (predictor-corrector and probability flow ODE), exact likelihood computation, and extensions to controllable generation. The work is well-structured, clearly written, and has had substantial impact on subsequent research in generative modeling.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient justification for score estimation accuracy in low-data-density regions: The paper assumes that the score function $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_t(\mathbf{x})$ can be accurately estimated across all time steps and data regions, particularly in low-density areas where $p_t(\mathbf{x})$ is small. While denoising score matching is used to train the score network, the stability of these estimates—especially at intermediate and late times when data is heavily corrupted—is not empirically validated or theoretically bounded. This is a critical assumption because the reverse SDE (Eq. 6) depends directly on the score, and errors in low-density regions can propagate and distort the entire sampling trajectory. The paper would benefit from ablation studies or sensitivity analyses showing how score estimation error at different noise levels affects final sample quality.

- Numerical stability of reverse-time SDE simulation not adequately addressed: The reverse-time SDE in Eq. (6) involves subtracting a term proportional to $g(t)^2 \nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_t(\mathbf{x})$, which can become large when the score is poorly estimated or when $g(t)$ is large (e.g., at early stages of the reverse process). The paper does not discuss potential numerical instabilities arising from this term, nor does it provide guarantees on the well-posedness of the discretized reverse process under finite step sizes. Given that sampling quality in Table 1 varies significantly across solvers, a more detailed analysis of solver stability—such as step size constraints or adaptive step selection—is warranted to ensure reliable deployment.

- Lack of comparison between ODE and SDE sampling trajectories in practice: The paper promotes the probability flow ODE (Eq. 13) as a deterministic alternative to the reverse SDE, enabling exact likelihoods and faster sampling. However, it does not demonstrate whether the two processes actually produce samples from the same distribution in finite-time simulations with imperfect scores. Prior work has shown gaps between ODE and SDE trajectories due to discretization and approximation errors. A direct comparison—e.g., via FID or visual inspection—of samples generated by both methods using the same score model would strengthen the claim that they are equivalent in practice, not just in theory.

- Overlooked dependence of performance on noise schedule design: While the paper introduces VE, VP, and sub-VP SDEs, it does not systematically analyze how the choice of $\beta(t)$ or $\sigma(t)$ affects score estimation accuracy, sampling efficiency, or final performance. The sub-VP SDE is introduced with a complex diffusion coefficient, but no ablation is provided to isolate its contribution from architectural changes or training objectives. Given that noise scheduling is a known lever for performance in diffusion models, the paper should include a controlled study varying the noise schedule while holding other factors constant to clarify what drives the reported improvements.

- Empirical evaluation lacks diversity in data modalities and inverse problem benchmarks: The experiments focus almost exclusively on image generation (CIFAR-10, LSUN, CelebA-HQ), with only qualitative results for inpainting and colorization. The claims about general controllable generation via Eq. (14) are not supported by quantitative benchmarks on standard inverse problems such as inpainting with missing rate variation, super-resolution, or compressed sensing. Including metrics like PSNR or LPIPS for reconstruction tasks would better substantiate the flexibility of the framework beyond unconditional generation.

- No ablation isolating sub-VP SDE contribution: The paper claims the sub-VP SDE enables superior likelihoods, culminating in a 2.99 bits/dim result. However, the best likelihoods are achieved only after combining the sub-VP SDE with architectural changes (deeper network) and the continuous training objective. There is no ablation that isolates the effect of the sub-VP SDE itself. To validate its contribution, the authors should report bits/dim for a shallow model trained with the discrete objective using the sub-VP SDE, keeping all other factors equal to the VP SDE baseline. Without this, it is unclear whether the gains come from the SDE design or from other improvements.

- Missing comparison to deterministic sampling in practice: The paper asserts that the probability flow ODE generates samples from the same distribution as the reverse-time SDE, but this equivalence is not empirically verified under realistic conditions. Readers will wonder whether discretization and score approximation errors break this equivalence. The authors should generate samples from both the ODE and SDE using the same trained score model and compute their FID scores relative to data. A large discrepancy would undermine the claim of equivalence, while close scores would strengthen it. This comparison is standard in the neural ODE literature and necessary here.

- No analysis of score estimation error propagation: The reverse SDE depends critically on accurate score estimates, yet the paper does not examine how errors in score estimation—especially at high noise levels or in low-density regions—affect sampling trajectories. A natural diagnostic is to inject controlled noise into the score network outputs during sampling and measure the degradation in FID or visual quality. Alternatively, the authors could compute the norm of score residuals on a held-out set across time steps. This would demonstrate awareness of a key failure mode and provide insight into robustness.

- Unverified claim of unified SMLD and DDPM framework: The paper states that SMLD and DDPM are discretizations of the VE and VP SDEs, but it does not verify that simulating the continuous SDEs recovers the behavior of the discrete models. To substantiate this unification claim, the authors should simulate the VE SDE with Euler-Maruyama and show that it reproduces the noise corruption process of SMLD with a fine discretization. Similarly, they should derive the ancestral sampling rule from the VP SDE and confirm it matches DDPM. Without this, the unification remains asserted, not demonstrated.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide ablation studies on score estimation accuracy across noise levels, particularly in low-density regions, and analyze its impact on sampling fidelity.

- Include a stability analysis of the reverse-time SDE discretization, with discussion of step size limits and error propagation.

- Add a direct comparison between samples generated by the probability flow ODE and the reverse SDE using the same trained score model, to validate their practical equivalence.

- Conduct a controlled study isolating the effect of SDE type and noise schedule on performance, disentangling it from architectural improvements.

- Expand empirical evaluation to include quantitative results on standard inverse problems (e.g., inpainting, super-resolution) to support claims of broad applicability.

comments.title: Malformed exponent in sub-VP SDE definition
comments.quote: \mathrm{d}\mathbf{x}=-\frac{1}{2}\beta(t)\mathbf{x}\;\mathrm{d}t+\sqrt{\beta(t)(1-e^{-2\frac{\mathrm{t}^{t}}{\mathrm{t}_{0}}\beta(s)\mathrm{d}s})}\mathrm{d}\mathbf{w}.
comments.explanation: The expression $\frac{\mathrm{t}^{t}}{\mathrm{t}_{0}}\beta(s)\mathrm{d}s$ is mathematically ill-defined: $\mathrm{t}^t$ is not standard notation, $\mathrm{t}_0$ is undefined, and $\mathrm{d}s$ appears outside an integral context. Given the intended behavior of the sub-VP SDE—bounding variance relative to the VP SDE—the diffusion coefficient must depend on the cumulative integral of $\beta(s)$. The correct form should be $\sqrt{\beta(t)\left(1 - e^{-2\int_0^t \beta(s)\,\mathrm{d}s}\right)}$. This correction ensures the SDE is properly specified and its properties can be derived.

comments.title: Incorrect proportionality in score norm weighting
comments.quote: Notably, the weights of the $i$-th summand in Eq. (1) and Eq. (3), namely $\sigma_{i}^{2}$ and $(1-\alpha_{i})$, are related to corresponding perturbation kernels in the same functional form: $\sigma_{i}^{2}\propto 1/\mathbb{E}[\|\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\log p_{\sigma_{i}}(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}\mid\mathbf{x})\|_{2}^{2}]$ and $(1-\alpha_{i})\propto 1/\mathbb{E}[\|\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\log p_{\alpha_{i}}(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}\mid\mathbf{x})\|_{2}^{2}]$.
comments.explanation:
The stated proportionality is inverted. For the SMLD case, $\mathbb{E}[\|
abla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_{\sigma_i}(	ilde{\mathbf{x}} \mid \mathbf{x})\|_2^2] = d / \sigma_i^2$, so $\sigma_i^2 \propto 1 / \mathbb{E}[\|
abla \log p\|^2]$ is correct only if interpreted as $\sigma_i^2 \propto \left(\mathbb{E}[\|
abla \log p\|^2]ight)^{-1}$. However, the phrasing suggests a direct inverse proportionality that obscures the actual relationship. More critically, for DDPM, $\mathbb{E}[\|
abla \log p\|^2] = d \alpha_i / (1 - \alpha_i)$, so $1 - \alpha_i \propto 1 / \mathbb{E}[\|
abla \log p\|^2]$ only if $\alpha_i$ is constant, which it is not. The weights are better understood as proportional to the noise variance, not inversely proportional to the expected score norm. Clarify that $\sigma_i^2$ and $1 - \alpha_i$ are proportional to the marginal variances of the perturbations.

comments.title: Overstatement of reverse-time SDE dependence on score alone
comments.quote: Crucially, the reverse-time SDE depends only on the time-dependent gradient field (a.k.a., score) of the perturbed data distribution.
comments.explanation: This statement is misleading. While the score appears in the drift term, the reverse-time SDE (Eq. 6) also depends explicitly on the drift $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}, t)$ and diffusion $g(t)$ of the forward SDE. The full dynamics are $\mathrm{d}\mathbf{x} = [\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}, t) - g(t)^2 \nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_t(\mathbf{x})] \mathrm{d}t + g(t) \mathrm{d}\bar{\mathbf{w}}$, so the coefficients $\mathbf{f}$ and $g$ are essential components. The word "only" incorrectly suggests the score is the sole dependency. Revise to acknowledge that the reverse SDE depends on both the score and the forward SDE coefficients.

comments.title: Misleading claim about ancestral sampling as SDE discretization
comments.quote: Ancestral sampling, the sampling method of DDPM (Eq. (4)), actually corresponds to one special discretization of the reverse-time VP SDE (Eq. (11)) (see Appendix E).
comments.explanation: This phrasing suggests a direct numerical correspondence, but ancestral sampling is not a standard discretization like Euler-Maruyama. It uses a fixed variance schedule and reparameterizes the reverse step using a predicted mean and pre-specified variance, which aligns with the reverse SDE only under specific modeling assumptions (e.g., perfect score estimation and matching of variance parameters). The equivalence is statistical, not numerical. A more accurate statement would be that ancestral sampling induces a reverse process that approximates the solution of the reverse-time SDE under certain conditions on the noise schedule and variance parameterization.

comments.title: Incorrect attribution of reverse SDE form to Anderson (1982)
comments.quote: Crucially, this reverse process satisfies a reverse-time SDE (Anderson, 1982), which can be derived from the forward SDE given the score of the marginal probability densities as a function of time.
comments.explanation: Anderson (1982) derives the general form of the time-reversed diffusion process, but does not explicitly connect the reverse drift to the score $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_t(\mathbf{x})$. That connection is critical to the method and was developed later in the context of score-based modeling (e.g., Hyvärinen, 2005; Welling & Teh, 2011). The paper should clarify that while the general reverse process form is due to Anderson, the specific use of the marginal score for generative modeling is a later innovation.

comments.title: Overstated claim of first 1024×1024 score-based generation
comments.quote: as well as high-fidelity generation of  $1024 \times 1024$  images for the first time from a score-based model.
comments.explanation: This claim is factually incorrect. NCSNv2 (Song & Ermon, 2020) already demonstrated $1024 \times 1024$ image generation on CelebA-HQ using a score-based model with multi-scale architectures and annealed Langevin dynamics, prior to this work. The authors may intend to claim novelty within their SDE-based framework, but as written, the statement overreaches. Qualify the claim to specify the methodological context, e.g., "using reverse-time SDE sampling".

comments.title: Flawed justification for score matching objective
comments.quote: With sufficient data and model capacity, score matching ensures that the optimal solution to Eq. (7), denoted by  $\mathbf{s}_{\theta^*}(\mathbf{x},t)$ , equals  $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\log p_t(\mathbf{x})$  for almost all  $\mathbf{x}$  and  $t$ .
comments.explanation: This claim conflates the marginal score $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_t(\mathbf{x})$ with the conditional expectation $\mathbb{E}[\nabla_{\mathbf{x}(t)} \log p_{0t}(\mathbf{x}(t) \mid \mathbf{x}(0)) \mid \mathbf{x}(t)]$, which are not equal in general. The optimal $\mathbf{s}_\theta$ from Eq. (7) converges to the latter, which equals the former only under restrictive conditions (e.g., Gaussian transition kernels with constant variance). For general SDEs, this identity fails. The justification for using Eq. (7) to estimate the marginal score is therefore incomplete. Revise to clarify that the objective consistently estimates the marginal score only under additional assumptions.

comments.title: Unjustified weighting function choice
comments.quote: As in SMLD and DDPM, we can typically choose  $\lambda \propto 1 / \mathbb{E}\big[\big\| \nabla_{\mathbf{x}(t)}\log p_{0t}(\mathbf{x}(t)\mid \mathbf{x}(0))\big\| _2^2\big]$ .
comments.explanation: The proposed weighting lacks theoretical grounding. In practice, $\lambda(t)$ is chosen to balance signal-to-noise across time steps and is typically proportional to $g(t)^2$ or $\beta(t)$, not the inverse of the expected squared conditional score norm. For example, in the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process, $\mathbb{E}[\|\nabla \log p_{0t}(\cdot \mid \mathbf{x}(0))\|^2]$ does not match standard choices. The justification misattributes the source of the weighting and could mislead readers. Replace with a more accurate description: $\lambda(t)$ is often proportional to the square of the diffusion coefficient or the noise schedule.

comments.title: Overgeneralization of Gaussian transition kernels
comments.quote: When $\mathbf{f}(\cdot,t)$ is affine, the transition kernel is always a Gaussian distribution, where the mean and variance are often known in closed-forms and can be obtained with standard techniques (see Section 5.5 in *Sárkkä and Solin (2019)*).
comments.explanation: The claim that affine drift implies Gaussian kernels 'always' is incorrect without assuming additive noise. If the diffusion coefficient $g(t, \mathbf{x})$ is state-dependent, the solution is generally non-Gaussian. The reference to Sárkkä and Solin (2019) applies only to linear SDEs with additive noise. The statement should be revised to require that $g(t)$ is constant in $\mathbf{x}$ for the kernel to be Gaussian.

comments.title: Missing regularity conditions for ODE solution existence
comments.quote:
This deterministic process satisfies an ODE (more details in Appendix D.1):

$$
\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} = \left[ \mathbf {f} (\mathbf {x}, t) - \frac {1}{2} g (t) ^ {2} \nabla_ {\mathbf {x}} \log p _ {t} (\mathbf {x}) \right] \mathrm {d} t, \tag {13}
$$
comments.explanation: The existence and uniqueness of solutions to Eq. (13) require the vector field to be locally Lipschitz in $\mathbf{x}$ and continuous in $t$. While $\mathbf{f}$ and $g(t)$ may satisfy these, $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_t(\mathbf{x})$ is a learned function whose regularity is not guaranteed. For example, ReLU-based networks can produce non-Lipschitz gradients, violating Picard-Lindelöf conditions. The paper should state that well-posedness depends on the smoothness of the score network and reference Appendix D.1 for verification under specific architectures.

comments.title: Inconsistent checkpoint selection undermines metric comparison
comments.quote: Results reported in Table 3 are for the checkpoint with the smallest FID over the course of training, where samples are generated with PC samplers. In contrast, FID scores and NLL values in Table 2 are reported for the last training checkpoint, and samples are obtained with black-box ODE solvers.
comments.explanation: Using different checkpoint selection criteria across tables introduces a confounding variable. Table 3 uses the best FID checkpoint, while Table 2 uses the final checkpoint, making cross-table comparisons unreliable. A model's final checkpoint may have worse FID due to overfitting, so differences in performance could reflect training dynamics rather than model design. To enable fair evaluation, all metrics should be computed on the same checkpoint. Clarify whether comparisons are valid or recompute results consistently.

comments.title: Conflation of architectural and objective improvements
comments.quote: By switching to the continuous training objective in Eq. (7), and increasing the network depth, we can further improve sample quality for all models. The resulting architectures are denoted as NCSN++ cont. and DDPM++ cont. in Table 3 for VE and VP/sub-VP SDEs respectively.
comments.explanation: The text attributes improved performance to two changes simultaneously—continuous objective and increased depth—without disentangling their contributions. The naming convention (e.g., 'NCSN++ cont.') bundles both, obscuring ablation. Without separate evaluation of each factor, it is impossible to determine whether gains come from architecture, training formulation, or interaction. To support the claim, report ablation results for: (1) same depth with continuous objective; (2) increased depth with discrete objective; (3) both.

comments.title: Overstatement of uniquely identifiable encoding
comments.quote: Uniquely identifiable encoding Unlike most current invertible models, our encoding is uniquely identifiable, meaning that with sufficient training data, model capacity, and optimization accuracy, the encoding for an input is uniquely determined by the data distribution (Roeder et al., 2020).
comments.explanation: The justification is incomplete. While the forward SDE has no trainable parameters, the reverse ODE (Eq. 13) depends on the learned score function, which has trainable weights. Different training runs could yield different encodings, violating identifiability in practice. Moreover, uniqueness requires the vector field to be locally Lipschitz, which is not verified. The claim holds only under idealized conditions of perfect score estimation and smoothness. Revise to clarify these assumptions or reference Appendix D.5 for empirical verification.

comments.title: Misleading claim of 'exact likelihood' computation
comments.quote: Exact likelihood computation Leveraging the connection to neural ODEs, we can compute the density defined by Eq. (13) via the instantaneous change of variables formula (Chen et al., 2018). This allows us to compute the exact likelihood on any input data (details in Appendix D.2).
comments.explanation: The term 'exact likelihood' is misleading. While the change of variables formula is exact in theory, the computation depends on a neural network approximation of the score. Errors in score estimation—especially at low noise levels—propagate through the ODE integration, biasing the final density. The computed likelihood is model-based, not ground-truth exact. Replace 'exact likelihood' with 'model-based likelihood computation' and clarify that accuracy depends on score fidelity.

comments.title: Incorrect conditioning in reverse-time SDE for inverse problems
comments.quote:
erse-time SDE:

$$
\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} = \left\{\mathbf {f} (\mathbf {x}, t) - g (t) ^ {2} \left[ \nabla_ {\mathbf {x}} \log p _ {t} (\mathbf {x}) + \nabla_ {\mathbf {x}} \log p _ {t} (\mathbf {y} \mid \mathbf {x}) \right] \right\} \mathrm {d} t + g (t) \mathrm {d} \bar {\mathbf {w}}. \tag
comments.explanation:
The expression adds $
abla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_t(\mathbf{x})$ and $
abla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_t(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x})$, implying $
abla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_t(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$, but this decomposition requires regularity and consistent support. More critically, $p_t(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x})$ is an external model, and its gradient may not align with the score of the generative model. This can lead to conflicting drift terms. The correct form should be $\mathrm{d}\mathbf{x} = \left[ \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}, t) - g(t)^2 
abla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_t(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{y}) ight] \mathrm{d}t + g(t) \mathrm{d} ar{\mathbf{w}}$, with the understanding that the posterior score must be consistently estimated.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces a unified framework for score-based generative modeling using stochastic differential equations (SDEs). The work unifies previous approaches (SMLD and DDPM) under a continuous-time formulation, enabling new sampling methods, exact likelihood computation, and controllable generation. The paper demonstrates state-of-the-art results on CIFAR-10 and high-fidelity 1024x1024 image generation.

The paper makes a significant theoretical contribution by unifying score-based generative modeling under the SDE framework. The mathematical derivations appear sound, and the empirical results are impressive, achieving new state-of-the-art on CIFAR-10. The framework elegantly connects to neural ODEs and enables both stochastic and deterministic sampling. The controllable generation capabilities through conditional reverse-time SDEs are particularly valuable.

Overview issues:

- Numerical solver stability and error accumulation: The paper extensively discusses numerical solvers for the reverse-time SDE but lacks theoretical analysis of discretization error propagation. The predictor-corrector framework helps mitigate errors, but there's no guarantee that accumulated numerical errors won't significantly affect sample quality, especially for long integration times. The paper should analyze the stability conditions for the proposed solvers and quantify how discretization errors impact the final distribution. This matters because the theoretical guarantees rely on exact integration of the reverse SDE, which is impossible in practice.

- Score estimation accuracy across diffusion timeline: The paper assumes the trained score network accurately estimates scores across all time steps t ∈ [0,T], but doesn't address the varying difficulty of score estimation at different noise levels. At early times (low noise), scores are high-frequency and hard to estimate; at late times (high noise), the signal is weak. The continuous objective in Eq. (7) weights all time steps equally via λ(t), which may not be optimal. The paper should analyze score estimation error as a function of t and its impact on sample quality. This is crucial because errors in score estimation directly propagate to the reverse SDE integration.

- Computational efficiency vs sample quality trade-off: While the paper demonstrates impressive sample quality, the computational cost remains high. Table 1 shows that even with 2000 steps, FID scores are still worse than GANs. The probability flow ODE offers faster sampling but requires careful tuning of error tolerance. The paper lacks a systematic analysis of the computational-accuracy trade-off across different solvers and step sizes. Readers need guidance on practical configurations for different use cases. This matters for real-world adoption where sampling speed is critical.

- Boundary conditions and manifold hypothesis: The SDE framework assumes data lives in ℝ^d, but real data often lies on low-dimensional manifolds. The paper doesn't address how the SDE behaves near manifold boundaries or whether the score remains well-defined when data is perturbed off the manifold. The Gaussian prior assumption may be inappropriate for data with bounded support (e.g., normalized images). The paper should discuss these limitations and potential solutions, such as projecting back to the manifold or using alternative priors. This affects the theoretical validity of the approach for constrained data types.

- Missing concrete SDE example computation: The paper introduces VE, VP, and sub-VP SDEs but never computes their key quantities for a concrete data distribution. Readers would benefit from seeing the forward SDE, reverse-time SDE, and probability flow ODE explicitly worked out for a simple case like a 2D Gaussian mixture. This would demonstrate how the score function looks at different times, how the reverse process works, and verify that the probability flow ODE indeed preserves marginals. Without such a worked example, the theoretical framework remains abstract and it's hard to build intuition about how the method behaves in practice.

- No demonstration of conditional reverse SDE derivation: Section 5 claims the conditional reverse-time SDE (Eq. 14) enables controllable generation, but the paper never shows how to derive the gradient term ∇ₓ log pₜ(y|x(t)) for any concrete application. For class-conditional generation, the paper mentions training a classifier but doesn't show how to compute its gradient with respect to x(t). For inpainting, the appendix is referenced but the main text lacks even a simple example showing how to derive the gradient for masking known pixels. This makes the conditional generation claims feel underdeveloped since readers cannot verify the approach without consulting appendices.

- Unverified connection to discrete SMLD/DDPM: The paper claims that SMLD and DDPM are discretizations of VE and VP SDEs respectively, but never demonstrates that the discrete reverse processes (Eqs. 2 and 4) are actually obtained by discretizing the continuous reverse-time SDE (Eq. 6). A concrete derivation showing how Eq. 6 reduces to Eq. 2 for VE SDE with specific discretization choices would strengthen this claim. Similarly for DDPM. Without this verification, the unification claim remains unsubstantiated and readers cannot see exactly how the continuous framework generalizes the discrete methods.

- Missing analysis of score estimation error propagation: The paper trains score networks to approximate ∇ₓ log pₜ(x) but doesn't analyze how estimation errors affect the sample quality. There should be at least a simple theoretical analysis or empirical study showing how errors in the score function propagate through the reverse SDE integration. For example, one could analyze the effect of a bounded score error on the final distribution's KL divergence from the target. This matters because the entire framework depends on accurate score estimation, yet the paper provides no insight into how robust the sampling process is to score estimation errors.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add theoretical analysis of numerical solver stability and error bounds for the reverse-time SDE integration

- Include empirical analysis of score estimation error across different time steps and its impact on sample quality

- Provide systematic evaluation of computational efficiency vs sample quality trade-offs across different solvers

- Discuss limitations related to the manifold hypothesis and bounded data support, with potential solutions

comments.explanation: The expression $\frac{\mathrm{t}^{t}}{\mathrm{t}_{0}}\beta(s)\mathrm{d}s$ in the exponential term is mathematically undefined. This appears to be a LaTeX rendering error. Based on the context of sub-VP SDEs, this should be an integral term. comments.explanation:
The paper provides no mathematical justification for why Eq. (13) preserves the same marginal densities as the original SDE. This is a fundamental claim that requires proof. Add the derivation: For an SDE $d\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x},t)dt + g(t)d\mathbf{w}$, the Fokker-Planck equation is $\partial_t p_t = -

(3) is also a weighted sum of denoising score matching objectives, which implies that the optimal model, $\mathbf{s}_{\bm{\theta}^{*}}(\tilde{\mathbf{x}},i)$, matches the score of the perturbed data distribution, $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\log p_{\alpha_{i}}(\mathbf{x})$.

Equation (3) minimizes the conditional score $

abla_{\mathbf{x}}\log p_{\alpha_{i}}(\mathbf{x})$. The bridge between conditional and marginal scores requires justification. Add the derivation: "Since $

comments.title: Reverse-time ODE for decoding not specified
comments.quote: Manipulating latent representations By integrating Eq. (13), we can encode any datapoint  $\mathbf{x}(0)$  into a latent space  $\mathbf{x}(T)$ . Decoding can be achieved by integrating a corresponding ODE for the reverse-time SDE.
comments.explanation: The reverse-time ODE for decoding is not specified. Given the forward-time probability flow ODE (Eq. 13), the reverse-time ODE should be $d\mathbf{x} = -[\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x},t) - \frac{1}{2}g(t)^2 \nabla_\mathbf{x} \log p_t(\mathbf{x})]dt$ when integrating from $T$ to $0$. This is critical since readers cannot implement the decoding process without this explicit form. Add: "Specifically, the reverse-time ODE is $d\mathbf{x} = -[\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x},t) - \frac{1}{2}g(t)^2 \nabla_\mathbf{x} \log p_t(\mathbf{x})]dt$, which is integrated from $t=T$ to $t=0$ for decoding."

comments.title: Imputation sampling method unclear
comments.quote: Imputation is a special case of conditional sampling. Suppose we have an incomplete data point  $\mathbf{y}$  where only some subset,  $\Omega(\mathbf{y})$  is known. Imputation amounts to sampling from  $p(\mathbf{x}(0) \mid \Omega(\mathbf{y}))$ , which we can accomplish using an unconditional model (see Appendix I.2).
comments.explanation:
The claim that imputation uses an "unconditional model" is misleading. While the score model $
abla_x \log p_t(x)$ is unconditional, the conditional gradient $
abla_x \log p_t(y|x)$ must still be computed. For imputation, this gradient enforces known pixel constraints: $
abla_x \log p_t(y|x) = \lambda \cdot M \odot (x - y)$ where $M$ is a binary mask for known pixels and $\lambda$ controls constraint strength. Replace "unconditional model" with "unconditional score model" and add: "where the conditional gradient term enforces known pixel constraints through masking".

(6) as $\mathrm{d}\mathbf{x} = [-\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}, t) + g(t)^2 \nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_t(\mathbf{x})] \mathrm{d}t + g(t) \mathrm{d}\bar{\mathbf{w}}$ where $\mathrm{d}t > 0$ represents backward time flow, or explicitly state that $\mathrm{d}t < 0$ and keep the current drift term.

For DDPM, $(1-\alpha_{i})$ is the variance of the perturbation kernel, but the claimed inverse relationship with the expected squared norm of the conditional score requires justification. Add the derivation: "Since $\mathbb{E}[\|\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\log p_{\alpha_{i}}(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}\mid\mathbf{x})\|_{2}^{2}] = \frac{d}{1-\alpha_{i}}$ for $d$-dimensional Gaussian noise, we have $(1-\alpha_{i}) \propto 1/\mathbb{E}[\|\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\log p_{\alpha_{i}}(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}\mid\mathbf{x})\|_{2}^{2}]$" after the quoted text.

Specify: "We convert RGB images to YUV color space, where Y (luminance) is known and U,V (chrominance) are unknown. The YUV transformation matrix is orthogonal, allowing direct imputation in this space."
